label: Glucosamine can be Hydrolysed from Chitin
title: Chitin, a natural polymer found in Crustacean Shells such as Shrimp and Crabs, can be chemically or enzymatically broken down and deacetylated to produce Glucosamine, a compound widely used in Joint Health Supplements.

label: Glucosamine can be Fermented from Corn
title: Glucosamine can also be produced from Corn through microbial fermentation, where corn-derived Glucose is converted into Glucosamine. This method provides a non–shellfish alternative often marketed as suitable for vegans and people with shellfish allergies.

label: Collagen (Type I, V, X) can be Extracted from Eggshell Membrane
title: Eggshell Membrane (ESM) is a natural, sustainable, vegetarian-friendly source of Collagen Type I, V, X and other joint-supporting compounds. ESM is becoming a more popular alternative source for Collagen to Bovine and Marine sources.

label: Calcium Carbonate (CaCO3) can be Extracted from Eggshells
title: Eggshells are composed of around 95–97% Calcium Carbonate and are typically cleaned, heat-treated (boiled or baked), and ground into a fine powder for further use.

label: Calcium Carbonate (CaCO3) can be Processed from Limestone
title: Calcium Carbonate is produced from Limestone primarily through mechanical crushing and chemical processing (precipitation) to produce high-purity Precipitated Calcium Carbonate

label: Calcium Carbonate (CaCO3) can be Processed from Chalk
title: Calcium Carbonate can be produced from ground Chalk as Ground Calcium Carbonate (GCC), or further processed through calcination and carbonation to form purer Precipitated Calcium Carbonate (PCC).

label: Calcium Carbonate (CaCO3) can be Processed from Marble
title: Calcium Carbonate can be produced from Marble dust or powder as a waste-derived source, then further chemically processed to obtain higher-purity Precipitated Calcium Carbonate (PCC)

label: Chondroitin can be Hydrolysed from Animal Cartilage
title: Animal Cartilage from land animals such as Cattle and Pigs is commonly processed through enzymatic hydrolysis and purification to extract Chondroitin Sulfate, a compound widely used in Joint Health Supplements.

label: Chondroitin can be Hydrolysed from Shark Cartilage
title: Shark Cartilage is a traditional source of Chondroitin Sulfate, obtained through multi-step extraction and purification processes. It has been widely used in supplements, though its sourcing raises conservation and sustainability concerns.

label: Derivative
title: Gelatin is produced by breaking down Collagen (primarily type I) from the skins/hides, bones, and connective tissues of Pigs, Cattle, or Avian sources through heat and controlled processing, converting it into a soluble, gel-forming protein.

label: Collagen (Type I, III) can be Extracted from Bovine Hides
title: Bovine Hides are rich in Type I Collagen, and also contain significant amounts of Collagen Type III, Bovine Collagen is often used in Supplements due to its similarity to Human Collagen.

label: Collagen (Type I, III) can be Extracted from Bovine Connective Tissues (Tendons, Ligaments, and Fascia)
title: Bovine Connective Tissues are primarily composed of Type I and Type III Collagen, commonly processed into Gelatin or Hydrolyzed Collagen for Supplements.

label: Collagen (Type I, III) can be Extracted from Porcine Bones
title: Collagen extracted from Porcine Bones is primarily Type I Collagen, often accompanied by smaller amounts of Type III Collagen. It is commonly used to make Gelatin, Collagen Peptides, food ingredients, and nutraceutical products.

label: Collagen (Type I, III) can be Extracted from Avian Skin
title: Avian Skins, especially Chicken, contain Type I and III Collagen, processed into Broth Products, Collagen Peptides, and limited Cosmetic and Food Applications.

label: Collagen (Type I, III) can be Extracted from Pig Skin
title: Pig Skin is a major commercial source of Type I and Type III Collagen. It is highly bioavailable, rich in glycine and proline, and is widely processed into Food-Grade Collagen, Gelatin, Sausage Casings, and Cosmetic or Pharmaceutical Collagen products.

label: Collagen (Type I, III) can be Extracted from Porcine Connective Tissues (Tendons, Ligaments, and Fascia)
title: Porcine Connective Tissues, including tendons and fascia, are rich in Type I Collagen and are processed into Collagen Peptides, Joint Health Supplements, and functional food ingredients.

label: Collagen (Type I, III) can be Extracted from Bovine Bones
title: Animal Bones are cleaned and demineralised using acid or alkali treatments to release Type I Collagen, which can then be further processed into Collagen or Gelatin.

label: Marine Collagen (Type I) can be Extracted from Fish Scales & Skin
title: Marine Collagen is commonly extracted from Fish Scales and Skins, which are cleaned and treated with acid or enzymes to isolate Type I Collagen. These sources are widely used in Cosmetics, Wound Dressings, and skincare products due to their high bioavailability and marine origin.

label: Marine Collagen (Type I) can be Extracted from Fish Byproducts (heads, bones, fins, vertebrae, cartilage)
title: Marine Collagen is also extracted from Fish Byproducts such as heads, fins, bones, cartilage, and vertebrae. These materials are cooked or enzymatically processed to release Collagen, which is commonly used in Marine Collagen Powders, Collagen Peptides, and certain Pharmaceutical Products.

label: Marine Collagen (Type I) can be Extracted from Shark Cartilage
title: Shark Cartilage is a controversial source of Marine Collagen, often marketed as a high-value ingredient because it is rich in Type II Collagen, which is associated with joint and cartilage support.

label: MSM (Methylsulfonylmethane) can be Oxidised from DMSO (Dimethyl Sulfoxide)
title: MSM used in Dietary Supplements is typically synthesised by oxidising DMSO (Dimethyl Sulfoxide) through controlled chemical or electrochemical processes, producing a stable, bioavailable sulfur compound.

label: Hyaluronic Acid can be Extracted from Rooster Combs
title: Hyaluronic Acid (HA) has traditionally been extracted from connective tissues in Rooster Combs through purification and isolation processes, and was historically a primary commercial source.

label: Hyaluronic Acid can be Fermented from Bacteria (Streptococcus spp. or Bacillus spp.)
title: Today, Hyaluronic Acid is widely produced using Bacterial Fermentation, where selected Microorganisms synthesise HA in controlled bioreactors. This method is preferred for its scalability, purity, and suitability for Pharmaceuticals, Cosmetics, and Joint Health Supplements.

label: Surimi can be Processed from Fish Meat & Products
title: Surimi is made from Fish Meat, which is finely minced, repeatedly washed to remove fats and pigments, and blended with stabilisers to create a neutral-flavoured protein base used in products such as Crab Sticks and other seafood analogues.

label: Collagen (Type II) can be Extracted from Avian (Poultry) Bones
title: Poultry Bones (especially Chicken) are rich in Collagen Type II, commonly extracted from Sternum Bones and Cartilage for use in Joint Support Formulas.

label: Collagen (Type II) can be Extracted from Poultry Connective Tissues (Tendons, Ligaments, and Fascia)
title: Poultry Connective Tissues contain smaller amounts of Collagen (type II) and are less commonly used at industrial scale, but are still processed into Broths and Joint Support Formulas.

label: Collagen (Type II) can be Extracted from Animal Cartilage
title: Animal Cartilage (especially Chicken), is rich in Type II Collagen, often processed to extract Collagen for use in Joint Health Supplements and biomedical applications.
Monoculture Farming drives Excessive Fertilisation, as repeatedly growing the same crop exhausts soil nutrients and degrades the soil microbiome. (125)

label: Contributes to
title: Runoff of nutrients, especially Nitrogen and Phosphate from soil Fertilisers, can enter lakes, estuaries, and other water bodies, leading to Eutrophication. (133)

label: Drives
title: Energy Consumption is a primary driver of Climate Change, as its production accounts for the majority of Greenhouse Gas Emissions, mainly through the burning of fossil fuels. (136)

label: Results from
title: Soil Fertility Decline can result from Soil Contamination, as pollutants degrade soil structure and harm beneficial organisms, reducing agricultural productivity (165)

label: Contributes to
title: Soil Fertility Decline and Soil Erosion reinforce each other: The loss of organic matter reduces soil structure and makes soils more vulnerable to erosion, while erosion removes nutrient-rich topsoil, further diminishing soil fertility. (149)

label: Results in
title: Soil Fertility Decline reduces Agricultural Yields by limiting nutrient availability, weakening soil structure, and impairing crop growth and productivity (124)
Pesticides and Herbicides contribute to Soil Degradation by killing beneficial soil microbes, disrupting nutrient cycles, damaging soil structure, and leaving persistent residues. (138)
Monoculture Farming typically requires higher Pesticide & Herbicide Use because single-crop systems are more vulnerable to pests and disease outbreaks. (125)

label: Contributes to
title: Pesticides leach from fields into groundwater and surface water, elevating toxicity levels. (25, 122)

label: Contributes to
title: The use of agricultural Herbicides and Pesticides contributes to Antibiotic/Antimicrobial Resistance by promoting the development and spread of antibiotic-resistance genes in bacteria through selective pressure (205, 206)

label: Leads to
title: Repeated use of Pesticides and Herbicides drives natural selection and revolutionary adaptation. (128)

label: Contributes to
title: Herbicide residues persist in soil, reducing microbial diversity and affecting soil health in fields. (25, 121)

label: Contributes to
title: Pesticides can enter air through evaporation, spray drift during application, and wind erosion of contaminated soil (25, 122)

label: Contributes to
title: Pesticides can kill off beneficial insects, pollinators, non-target species, and disrupt soil fauna (120)

label: Leads to
title: Disease Outbreaks, such as Bird Flu or Foot and Mouth Disease, often lead to Mass Culling where millions of livestock are destroyed to prevent further spread (158)

label: Results in
title: Disease Outbreaks lead to interspecies or intraspecies Transmission, occurring directly or indirectly through interactions among the agent, host, and environment (159)
Monoculture Farming increases Water Overuse because uniform root systems reduce soil structure, water uptake, and water retention. (125)

label: Causes
title: Water Overuse, particularly in Agriculture, drives Ground Water Depletion by extracting water faster than natural recharge rates. (120)

Soil Fertility Decline is a component of Soil Degradation, where loss of organic matter weakens soil structure, increases compaction, reduces water infiltration, and disrupts beneficial soil organisms (124)

Monoculture Farming disrupts soil balance by depleting nutrients, reducing fertility, and accelerating Soil Erosion, as a single crop root system cannot adequately retain water or stabilize soil. (125)

label: Results in
title: Soil degradation weakens the soil's structure and reduces organic matter, making it more vulnerable to erosion by wind and water. (120, 124, 149)

label: Results in
title: Soil Degradation is a major challenge for Agriculture, as its cascading impacts have caused significant losses in Agricultural Yields (186)

label: Causes
title: Deforestation destroys habitats, causing widespread habitat loss and endangering many plant and animal species. (126,127)

label: Exacerbates
title: Deforestation worsens atmospheric carbon levels, as the loss of trees diminishes the capacity of forests to sequester CO2. (127)

label: Contributes to
title: Deforested areas lose the ability to regulate moisture and water cycles, altering weather patterns and contributing to Climate Change. (127)

label: Leads to
title: Deforestation leads to Soil Erosion as the loss of trees reduces water retention and destabilizes topsoil, particularly in coastal areas. (127)

label: Contributes to
title: Forests sustain the livelihoods of millions of people, communities, and national economies. Deforestation diminishes these resources, indirectly affecting the economy. (126)
Monoculture Farming causes significant Soil Fertility Decline by depleting nutrients and reducing beneficial microbes and organisms that maintain soil health. (141)

label: Contributes to
title: Monoculture reduces Biodiversity and leads to a less resilient and poorer ecosystem. (125)

label: Results in
title: Expansion of monocultures converts natural ecosystems, reducing habitat connectivity for wildlife. (120)
Energy Overconsumption, largely linked to Energy Production, accounts for roughly 75% of global GHG Emissions. (136)

label: Causes
title: Greenhouse Gases such as Carbon Dioxide, Methane, Nitrous Oxide, and certain synthetic chemicals trap outgoing heat in the atmosphere, altering climate and weather patterns. (135)

label: Leads to
title: Antibiotic Overuse or Misuse in humans, animals, and plants is a primary driver of Drug/Antibiotic/Antimicrobial Resistance in pathogens (204)

Heavy metals, diluted biocides, and pharmaceutical contaminants in waterways exert selective pressure on aquatic bacteria, promoting the development and spread of resistance through gene transfer, and contributing to Drug/Antibiotic/Antimicrobial Resistance (205, 206)
Water Contamination with excess nutrients such as Nitrogen and Phosphorus leads to Eutrophication, promoting Algal Blooms and Dead Zones (oxygen depletion). (133)
Mass Culling poses significant risks of Water Contamination through carcass burial, potentially allowing pathogens to leach into groundwater and nearby surface water sources. (162)

Water Contamination leads to Food Chain Bioaccumulation when aquatic organisms absorb persistent toxins such as methylmercury, PCBs, PFAS, microplastics, and heavy metals, which then biomagnify up the food chain, resulting in apex predators, including humans, having the highest concentrations and increased risk of toxicity (166)
Soil Contaminants, including Pesticides, Fertilisers, and Heavy Metals, can leach into groundwater and flow into surface waters. (130)
Pollutants released into the air—such as Sulfur, Nitrogen, and Heavy Metals—can enter rivers, lakes, and oceans through rain, snow, or airborne dust. (129)

label: Results in
title: Contaminated Water from human activities introduces microbes, chemicals, heavy metals, and radioactive elements into drinking water sources such as rivers, groundwater, and tanks, making it unsafe without proper treatment. (142)

label: Drives
title: Water Contamination harms both aquatic and terrestrial ecosystems. Pollutants directly damage aquatic life and can infiltrate soils, altering their properties and harming plants, creating cascading effects that can lead to Ecosystem Degradation or even Collapse. (144)

label: Results in
title: Water Contamination not only threatens drinking water, but also harms human health through non-drinking pathways, such as skin contact with polluted water and inhalation of contaminated droplets or aerosols. (142, 143)
Mass Culling using chemical methods, such as PFAS-based agents in poultry operations, carries risks of environmental Chemical Exposure due to their persistence and potential for bioaccumulation (163)
Toxic Substances released into the environment from human activities can accumulate in organisms through Bioaccumulation and biomagnify across the food chain, ultimately entering the human body (199)

label: Leads to
title: Environmental Chemical Exposure to toxic pollutants such as heavy metals, dioxins, pesticides, and benzene leads to Human Health Impacts through inhalation, ingestion, and bioaccumulation, resulting in respiratory diseases, cancers, metabolic disorders, neurodegenerative conditions, and increased mortality (199)

Mass Culling of Livestock can act as a point source for Antibiotic Resistance, as residual antibiotics in carcasses may leach into the environment and promote the gradual development of resistant microbial strains (164)

label: Causes
title: Antibiotic/Antimicrobial Resistance is one of the most significant global Public Health threats, directly causing ~1.27 million deaths in 2019 and contributing to ~4.95 million additional deaths globally (204)

label: Leads to
title: Antibiotic/Antimicrobial Resistance reduces treatment effectiveness, impacting Human Health by increasing transmission, severity of illness, risk of disability, and mortality (204)

label: Results in
title: Resistant Pests and Weeds reduce Agricultural Productivity by rendering standard treatments ineffective, increasing chemical use, and ultimately lowering Agricultural Yields

label: Leads to
title: Loss of Sea Ice, Glaciers, and Snow, particularly the melting of Arctic permafrost, can lead to widespread Ecosystem Loss, threatening associated wildlife and human communities (183)

label: Triggers
title: Eutrophication promotes rapid algal growth, which can include toxin-producing species that lead to Harmful Algal Blooms (HABs). (145)

label: Causes
title: Eutrophication (nutrient overload in water) acts as a fertiliser, allowing algae to grow and reproduce rapidly and form dense blooms. (145)

label: A result of
title: Wildfires and Bushfires can contribute to Eutrophication by depositing nutrient-rich ash containing elements such as nitrogen and phosphorus into aquatic systems. This nutrient input can stimulate excessive growth of algae and cyanobacteria, depleting dissolved oxygen levels, causing hypoxic conditions, and degrading water quality (181)
Mass Culling can contribute to the persistence of disease in the environment when carcasses are improperly buried or disposed of, creating long-term risks for other animals (163)

label: Leads to
title: Mass Culling leads to Production Shortages by removing large numbers of market-ready animals, disrupting supply chains and creating gaps in the consumer market.

label: Triggers
title: Mass Culling of Livestock presents major Ethical Dilemmas involving Animal Welfare, Worker Psychological Trauma, Economic Losses, and Public Health priorities, highlighting the need for improved management strategies and alternative methods. (158, 161)

label: Causes
title: Mass Culling of Livestock causes significant Economic Decline, ranging from immediate producer losses to broader market destabilisation, including supply chain disruptions, financial losses, and long-term market volatility.

label: Linked to
title: Flooding can drive Disease Transmission as standing water creates breeding grounds for disease-carrying insects like mosquitoes, while contaminated floodwaters spread waterborne illnesses such as Leptospirosis (176)

label: Results in
title: Serious Disease Transmission or Outbreaks can spread rapidly as epidemics or pandemics, threatening Human Life across multiple regions if not effectively contained and managed

label: Leads to
title: Disease Transmission or Outbreaks pose significant risks to Public Health and place substantial pressure on healthcare systems and local economies in affected communities

label: Results in
title: Disease Outbreaks require substantial resources across multiple sectors, placing strain on the economy. Pandemics such as COVID-19 can cause significant Economic Decline, with long-term disruptions to supply chains and labor markets (195)

label: Leads to
title: Toxic Substances and Chemical Contaminants such as Heavy Metals, PCBs, PBDEs, and Microplastics can enter the food web through bioaccumulation and biomagnification, accumulating in the tissues of organisms consumed by humans and adversely impacting Human Health, including organ damage, immune dysfunction, and physiological disorders (200)
Humans and animals can be exposed to hazardous chemicals from contaminated soil through multiple pathways, including direct contact, ingestion, and inhalation (167)

Soil Contaminated with Chemicals from Agricultural runoffs such as Pesticides and Herbicides can act as a training ground for bacteria and pathogens to build up antibiotic resistance genes, contributing to Drug/Antibiotic/Antimicrobial Resistance (205, 206)
Runoff of nutrients—particularly Nitrogen and Phosphorus from Fertilisers—can enter lakes, estuaries, and coastal waters, triggering Eutrophication. (133)
Mass Culling contributes to Soil Contamination as blood and tissue from culled animals can contaminate the soil and surrounding vegetation (162)
Contaminants from Soil can be taken up by crops and subsequently accumulate through the food chain, posing risks to humans and animals (165)

Soil Contamination contributes to Air Pollution through wind-borne dust carrying contaminated particles, vapor intrusion, physical disturbances such as farming or land clearing, and the emission of gases from polluted soils (167, 168)
Polluted Soil leads to Biodiversity Loss by eliminating sensitive species, reducing the diversity of soil organisms and plant life (165)

label: Leads to
title: Soil Contamination kills beneficial soil organisms, disrupts soil ecosystems and plant metabolism, stunting crop growth and leading to Reduced Agricultural Yields

label: Drives
title: Soil Contamination drives Ecosystem Degradation through cascading effects, including Biodiversity Loss, Soil Fertility Decline, and toxic accumulation in the food chain (169)

label: Threatens
title: Soil Contamination affects Human Health through long-term exposure to persistent chemicals via skin contact or inhalation of dust, contributing to organ damage, increased disease risk, and premature deaths, particularly among vulnerable populations (165)

label: Contributes to
title: Rising Global Temperatures cause seawater to warm and expand through thermal expansion, increasing its volume and contributing to Sea Level Rise (190)

label: Leads to
title: Global Temperature Rise contributes to an increase in Natural Disasters, including tropical Storms, Hurricanes, Cyclones, and Typhoons (191)

label: Links to
title: Ecosystem Degradation or Collapse and Global Warming form a positive feedback loop, where Ecosystem Degradation reduces the capacity of ecosystems to regulate global biogeochemical cycles, thereby accelerating Global Warming. In turn, Global Warming further intensifies Ecosystem Degradation (187)

label: Contributes to
title: Heatwaves contribute to Global Warming by increasing energy consumption (e.g., air conditioning demand) and intensifying heat retention in the atmosphere (173, 174)

label: Causes
title: Rising Global Temperatures are accelerating the melting of Sea Ice, Glaciers, and Snow, posing significant global risks as major ice regions (Greenland, Antarctica, and the Arctic) have been rapidly losing ice mass over the past decade, creating feedback loops that further intensify Global WarmingRising Global Temperatures are accelerating the melting of Sea Ice, Glaciers, and Snow, posing significant global risks as major ice regions (Greenland, Antarctica, and the Arctic) have been rapidly losing ice mass over the past decade, creating feedback loops that further intensify Global Warming (182)

label: Results in
title: Rising Global Temperature results in the melting of Sea Ice and Glaciers, which is particularly pronounced in the Arctic, where a large proportion of the oldest and thickest ice has been lost (182)

label: Contributes to
title: Rising Global Temperatures increase the risk and severity of Droughts by enhancing evaporation from soils, plants, and water bodies, thereby drying the land surface and increasing atmospheric aridity (192)

label: Exacerbates
title: Global Warming increases evaporation and atmospheric moisture, leading to more intense rainfall and a higher risk of Flooding (191)

label: Exacerbates
title: Global Warming intensifies Rainfall and Storms by increasing atmospheric moisture and energy, which raises the risk of Flooding (191)

label: Exacerbates
title: Rising Global Temperatures make Storms and Hurricanes more severe and, in many regions, more frequent by increasing atmospheric moisture and warming ocean surfaces that provide additional energy for their development (191)

label: Drives
title: Rising Global Temperatures drive Marine Heatwaves by increasing ocean heat uptake from solar radiation and enhancing the horizontal transport of heat through ocean currents (185)

Air Pollution can exacerbate Drug/Antibiotic/Antimicrobial Resistance by acting as a medium for the dispersal of bacteria and pathogens carrying resistance genes, enabling wider and more rapid spread (206)
Atmospheric deposition of Nitrogen adds excess nutrients to the Marine Environment, contributing to Eutrophication. (132)
Mass Culling contributes to Air Pollution when carcasses are burned or incinerated, releasing pollutants and particulate matter into the atmosphere (162)
Air Pollutants, especially Carbon Dioxide, increase acidity and lower pH levels in lakes, streams, and the ocean, altering habitats for aquatic flora and fauna. (129,131)

Wildfires and Bushfires can contribute significantly to Air Pollution, as ash and particulate matter from the fires can be dispersed into the environment and contaminate beaches, freshwater systems, water catchments, and drinking water sources (181)

label: Causes
title: Air Pollution impacts Human Health through exposure to atmospheric contaminants such as dust, fumes, gases, mists, odours, smoke, and vapours, increasing the risk of stroke, heart disease, respiratory disease, lung cancer, pneumonia, and cataracts, with major pollutants of concern including particulate matter (PM), carbon monoxide (CO), ozone (O3), nitrogen dioxide (NO2), and sulphur dioxide (SO2) (170)
Loss of Natural Ecosystems leads to Biodiversity Loss as organisms lose essential habitats, reducing their ability to survive, reproduce, and maintain stable populations
Habitat Loss and Fragmentation isolate populations and reduce available living space, increasing local extinctions and driving Biodiversity Loss. (134)

label: Leads to
title: Biodiversity Loss leads to the decline and extinction of essential pollinators, reducing Agricultural Yields and contributing to an estimated loss of up to $577 billion in annual global crop output (189)

label: Leads to
title: Biodiversity Loss reduces essential ecosystem services such as pollination, water purification, flood control, and soil fertility, creating cascading effects that can lead to Ecosystem Degradation or even Ecosystem Collapse. (147)

label: Links to
title: Biodiversity Loss and Ecosystem Degradation or Collapse are linked in a reinforcing feedback loop, where Biodiversity Loss weakens ecosystem stability and function, driving Degradation, while Ecosystem Degradation further reduces species populations and habitats, accelerating Biodiversity Loss (187)

label: Driven by
title: Melting Ice, Glaciers, and Snow drive Biodiversity Loss by destroying habitats of cold-adapted species and altering downstream river temperatures and chemistry, disrupting a broader range of species beyond cold-adapted ecosystems (193)

label: A result of
title: Wildfires directly destroy habitats and kill large numbers of wildlife, resulting in Biodiversity Loss (181)

label: Driven by
title: Biodiversity Loss is driven by Species Extinction, as each loss disrupts complex food webs and degrades ecosystems

label: Causes
title: Ocean Acidification affects a wide range of marine species but disproportionately impacts mollusks, corals, and echinoderms, reducing mollusk larval survival by up to 40%, lowering coral calcification by ~22–32%, and impairing echinoderm development (202)

label: Drives
title: Ocean Acidification reduces the ability of shell-forming organisms such as shellfish and corals to build shells and skeletons, disrupting complex marine food webs and driving Ecosystem Degradation or Collapse (201)
Global Temperature Rise (or Global Warming) is a primary component of Climate Change and a major driver of environmental impacts (135)

label: Results in
title: Climate Change increases the intensity and frequency of Natural Disasters and makes Weather Patterns more unpredictable, as rising temperatures enhance evaporation and atmospheric moisture, intensifying extreme rainfall and flooding, while warmer ocean surfaces fuel stronger storms, cyclones, hurricanes, and typhoons (191)

label: Exacerbates
title: Climate Change disrupts water availability, intensifying Water Scarcity and Water Shortages in already water-stressed regions, and increasing the risk and severity of Droughts (191)

label: Exacerbates
title: Climate Change intensifies and increases the frequency of Storms and Rainfall, raising the risk of Flooding (191)

label: Exacerbates
title: Climate Change drives more severe Storms and Hurricanes by warming ocean surfaces, which provide additional energy that fuels their intensity (191)

label: Drives
title: Climate Change and Wildfires have a circular relationship, in which Climate Change increases the frequency and intensity of Wildfires and Bushfires, while Wildfires and Bushfires also contribute to Climate Change by releasing greenhouse gases and reducing forests’ capacity to offset carbon emissions, creating a Climate Feedback Loop (181)

label: Contributes to
title: Climate Change has combined direct and indirect impacts on Human Health through cascading effects, including increased spread of infectious diseases, worsening air pollution, more frequent extreme weather events, and additional environmental and societal pressures (191)

label: Drives
title: Groundwater Depletion drives excessive groundwater extraction to meet demand, lowering water tables and allowing seawater to encroach into aquifers, resulting in Saltwater Intrusion (188)
Soil Erosion degrades natural habitats by removing topsoil and destabilizing land, contributing to Habitat Fragmentation and Habitat Loss (175)

label: Caused by
title: Sea Level Rise accelerates Soil Erosion by elevating baseline water levels, allowing waves and tides to reach further inland and exert greater erosive force on coastlines (175)

label: Contributes to
title: Soil Erosion, especially when combined with Flooding, can damage infrastructure by undermining foundations and destabilizing land, affecting roads, bridges, sewage systems, power plants, and other critical facilities (175)
Habitat Loss and Habitat Fragmentation can lead to broader Ecosystem Loss if not mitigated or managed effectively

label: A result of
title: Sea Ice, Glaciers, and Snow provide critical habitats for many species, and their melting leads to Habitat Loss and Habitat Fragmentation, disrupting ecosystems and the species that depend on them (183)

label: Top Consumer & Top Importer & Significant Producer
title: U.S. is the largest Consumer market for Joint Health Supplements (3). U.S. is the largest Importer of Joint Health Supplements (~33% of global shipments) (3). U.S. is a notable Producer of Joint Health Supplements (48, 67)

label: Top Producer
title: The US is a major Producer of Gummies, including both flavored confectionery and dietary supplement varieties (48, 107, 108)

label: Significant Consumer
title: The EU is a notable Consumer of Joint Health Supplements, ~20% of global market share (5)

label: Significant Exporter
title: Australia is a major Exporter of Joint Health Supplements (67)

label: Significant Producer
title: Germany is a traditional hub for confectionery production and a major Producer of gummy candies, best known for the brand Haribo (48, 107, 108)

label: Top Producer & Top Exporter
title: China is the largest Supplier and Manufacturer of Joint Health Supplements, ~80% of global production (1). China is the largest Exporter of Joint Health Supplements, also largest share in US imports (~77%) (2)

label: Top Producer
title: China is one of the largest Producers of gummies globally, leading in both flavored confectionery and dietary supplement varieties (48, 107, 108)

label: Significant Consumer & Significant Producer
title: Japan is a significant Consumer of Joint Health Supplements due to aging population (2). Japan is a major Producer of Joint Health Supplements in Asia-Pacific (2)

label: Recognised Producer
title: Japan is a notable Producer of Gummies, known for unique flavors and textures, producing both confectionery and dietary supplement varieties (48, 107)

label: Significant Producer & Significant Exporter
title: India is a major Producer and Exporter of Joint Health Supplements (4). India is a major Exporter of Joint Health Supplements (48, 67)

label: Significant Importer
title: Vietnam is a significant Importer of Joint Health Supplements (~18% of global shipments) (3, 67)

label: Global Leader & Top Exporter
title: The US has the largest Corn Farming industry, producing ~354.19M tonnes, with ~57.79M tonnes exported (2022-2023) (9, 38). The US is the largest Exporter of Corn, ~US$14.3B, 2024 (8)

label: Significant Grower
title: The US has a major Cattle Farming industry, with a large herd (~92.08M, 2022) and leading Beef Production despite a smaller head count than India, Brazil, and China (50)

label: Significant Processor
title: The US ranks among the top 5 in annual Cattle Slaughter, ~30.9M head (2025) (96)

label: Significant Grower
title: The US has a significant Pig Farming industry, ranking among the top producers globally, though far behind China, with a pig population of ~74.5M (2021-2025) (94, 95)

label: Recognised Processor
title: The US ranks among the top in Swine Slaughter, after China and the EU, with ~131.8M pigs killed/year (97)

label: Top Importer
title: The US is the leading Importer of Live Bovines by both value and head count (~$2.99B, ~2M head, 2024) (84)

label: Recognised Importer
title: The US, along with Spain, is a significant Livestock Bovine Importer (~$208.75M, ~4.88M head, 2024) (85)

label: Significant Exporter
title: The United States is a notable Exporter of live poultry/fowls—including chickens, ducks, geese, turkeys, and guinea fowls—ranking high by trade value (~$359.62M) with a lower export quantity (~81.75M head, 2024) (86, 87, 88, 89)

label: Significant Processor
title: The US ranks 2nd in Poultry Slaughter after China, with ~9.75B fowls/year (2023), incl. chickens, turkeys, ducks, though far behind in number (98)

label: Significant Catcher
title: The US has well-established lobster and shrimp fisheries (43)

label: Global Leader
title: The USA is the undisputed global leader in Biotechnology, leading in both firm numbers and R&D (48, 73, 74)

label: Significant Producer
title: The US is among the top global shrimp producers with ~837,622 tonnes (40, 41)

label: Significant Catcher
title: Indonesia ranks 2nd globally in Fisheries production, with 7.4M tonnes in 2022, about half of China's (75)

label: Top Catcher
