Heatwaves can significantly impact surface water quality and lead to Unsafe Drinking Water, as elevated air temperatures and drought conditions exceed ecosystem tolerance thresholds and degrade water quality, while associated extreme weather events can also increase surface runoff and flash flooding, carrying contaminants into water supplies (173, 174)
Floods can rapidly contaminate drinking water supplies, overwhelming treatment systems and washing pollutants like sewage, chemicals, and debris into both surface and ground water ()
Saltwater Intrusion into freshwater aquifers degrades water quality by increasing salinity and mobilizing contaminants, making Drinking Water Unsafe (188)
Increasing Natural Disasters and Irregular Weather Patterns disrupt growing conditions, damaging crops and reducing Agricultural Yields
Reduced Agricultural Yields directly impact the Food Production industry, leading to Food Production Shortages
Droughts reduce water availability and degrade water quality for agriculture, leading to lower Agricultural Yields
Floods can greatly reduce Agricultural Yields by inundating croplands, damaging crops, eroding fertile soil, and disrupting planting and harvesting cycles
Wildfires and Bushfires can destroy pasture, crops, fields, livestock, and vineyards, significantly impacting Agriculture and reducing Agricultural Yields (181)
Saltwater Intrusion directly reduces Agricultural Yields by increasing soil salinity, degrading soil health, and damaging or killing crops (188)
Water Shortages can reduce Agricultural Yields by limiting crop growth and productivity (180)
Rising Sea Levels increase the risk of Natural Disasters in coastal cities and small island nations, including Flooding, Storm Surges, and other extreme weather events (183)
Melting Glaciers driven by Global Warming add to Rising Sea levels with Greenland and Antarctic ice sheets are the largest contributors of Global Sea Level Rise (175, 182)
Sea Level Rise leads to more frequent and widespread Flooding, causing damages to infrastructure, disrupt daily life, and pose hazards to safety and property (175)
Sea Level Rise can trigger Saltwater Intrusion by introducing seawater to freshwater aquifers, degrading water quality and making it unusable for drinking and aquaculture (175)

Sea Ice and Glaciers melting can disrupt ocean circulation patterns, potentially contributing to changes in weather systems and increasing the frequency or intensity of extreme Natural Disasters such as storms and hurricanes (182)
Increases in Natural Disasters pose a major risk to Human Life, particularly in vulnerable regions with limited resources and low adaptive capacity
Harmful Algal Blooms can create Hypoxia and Dead Zones when decaying algae consume dissolved oxygen in the water. (145)
Algal Blooms may include or promote toxin-producing species, but not all blooms develop into Harmful Algal Blooms. (145)

Harmful Algal Blooms, often involving toxin-producing cyanobacteria in freshwater or dinoflagellates in marine environments, release toxins that are lethal to aquatic life, and prolonged or frequent events can result in Marine Biodiversity Loss (146)
Algal Blooms are a major driver of Hypoxia; when algae die and decompose, oxygen levels drop, forming Dead Zones. (145)
Hypoxia/Dead Zones directly cause mortality of aquatic life, threatening Biodiversity as low-oxygen conditions cannot support most fish and invertebrates (146)
Even non-toxic Algal Blooms can block sunlight, damage fish gills, and disrupt aquatic ecosystems, causing mortality of aquatic species and threatening Marine Biodiversity (145)
Coral Bleaching can lead to Reef Degradation, disrupting entire reef ecosystems, increasing mortality among marine organisms, and contributing to Biodiversity Loss (184)
Marine Heatwaves impose thermal stress on marine organisms, driving mortality of aquatic life, reducing species’ resilience and recovery capacity, and resulting in Biodiversity Loss (185)
Ecosystem Degradation or Collapse affects Agriculture by reducing populations of natural pollinators and increasing the prevalence of pests and diseases, ultimately reducing Agricultural Yields (186)

The Degradation or Collapse of major Ecosystems, such as the Amazon rainforest and the Great Barrier Reef, can disrupt the water cycle and weaken natural buffering systems against extreme events, altering Weather Patterns and increasing vulnerability to Natural Disasters such as severe Flooding, Storm Surges, and accelerated Erosion (187)
Toxins from Harmful Algal Blooms can poison wildlife and deplete oxygen in surrounding waters, destabilizing ecosystems and contributing to Ecosystem Degradation. (145)

Heatwaves can lead to Ecosystem Degradation or Collapse by disrupting vegetation and wildlife: extreme heat reduces plant photosynthesis and water balance, lowers food quality, increases reproductive failure, and raises mortality rates, ultimately weakening ecosystem stability (173)

Ecosystem Degradation or Collapse drives Species Extinction by progressively destroying and fragmenting habitats, reducing population sizes, disrupting ecological interactions, and ultimately leaving species unable to survive or reproduce (143, 187)

Water Scarcity is a major driver of Biodiversity Loss and the disappearance of Wetlands, undermining ecological resilience and leading to the loss and degradation of Ecosystems and their services (180)

Mass Coral Bleaching has already affected a large share of the world’s coral reefs, and with continuously rising Ocean Temperatures, corals are increasingly unable to recover to their healthy state—pushing reef ecosystems toward widespread degradation and potential collapse (185)

Ecosystem Degradation or Collapse can trigger cascading impacts on ecosystem goods and services, including reduced agricultural productivity, increased disease risk, and heightened exposure to natural disasters, thereby threatening Agriculture and Food Security (186)
Melting Glaciers, Ice, and Snow reduce the Earth’s ability to reflect solar radiation (albedo), leading to increased heat absorption and contributing to more intense heatwaves worldwide (183)
Heatwaves increase the risk of Bushfires and Wildfires, leading to Human Displacement, Habitat Destruction, and Air Pollution (173, 174)

Heatwaves are a leading cause of weather-related deaths, as extreme heat can trigger heatstroke and worsen pre-existing conditions, particularly among people with chronic diseases, older adults, and children (171)

Heatwaves can indirectly lead to Human Displacement through cascading impacts, where extreme heat intensifies hazards such as Droughts, Wildfires, and Floods, which in turn force populations to relocate (173)

Atmospheric Heatwaves can trigger and intensify Marine Heatwaves, where prolonged hot, low-wind conditions over the ocean reduce heat dissipation and increase sea surface temperatures over large areas (185)

Melting Ice, Glaciers, and Snow can threaten Arctic communities that depend on ecosystem services for their livelihoods, potentially forcing displacement as their local environment and habitats degrade or disappear (183)

Melting Glaciers lead to Water Shortages/Scarcity by reducing natural freshwater storage, causing a short-term surplus followed by long-term declines in water availability in rivers and lakes during dry periods (193)
Melting Ice can alter Global Shipping Routes both positively and negatively by opening new routes in the Arctic while also disrupting existing routes, presenting challenges for global logistics (183)
Melting Ice and Glaciers can force wild animals to move closer to human settlements in search of food and suitable habitat, often resulting in more frequent Human-Wildlife Conflicts (183)
Droughts can trigger Wildfires as vegetation becomes drier and more flammable, increasing the likelihood of ignition and enabling fires to spread more easily
Droughts are contributing to increasing Human Displacement, often within countries. This can strain local resources and exacerbate existing conflicts (179)
Prolonged droughts can lead to Water Scarcity, directly threatening the health, safety, and welfare of communities (177)
Floods pose a significant risk to Human Life by causing injuries and fatalities through drowning and structural collapse (176)
Flooding can lead to temporary or permanent Human Displacement, creating challenges such as Overcrowding, Social Inequalities, and Civil Unrest (176)
Wildfires and Bushfires can threaten Human Lives by releasing toxic smoke and particulate matter that exacerbate respiratory and cardiovascular diseases, potentially leading to premature death (198)

Species Extinction drives Biodiversity Loss and threatens entire Ecosystems. Over the past 500 years, 20–24 marine extinctions have been recorded, while widespread declines in marine populations may accelerate future extinctions and increase the risk of ecosystem degradation (203)

Heatwaves with increased frequency are particularly harmful to marine life, as repeated thermal stress disrupts ecosystems, causes mass mortality events, and increases the risk of Species Extinction (173)
Marine Heatwaves are responsible for Mass Coral Bleaching and significant declines in kelp forests and seagrass meadows, threatening the goods and services provided by these ecosystems (185)

Unsafe Drinking Water can cause short- and long-term health effects, ranging from gastrointestinal illness to cancer, depending on the contaminants. Chemical Exposure may lead to skin conditions, nervous system or organ damage, developmental or reproductive disorders, and cancer, while Microbial Contamination can spread diseases such as cholera, typhoid, hepatitis, and severe diarrheal illness. (142)
Harmful Algal Blooms (HABs) can affect human health through inhalation of toxin-containing aerosols released by certain algal species. (143)
Heatwaves increase the risk of heat stroke, severe dehydration, and acute cerebrovascular events, while also contributing to blood clot formation (171)
Droughts directly impact Human Health by reducing water quantity and quality, increasing the incidence of illness and disease, raising mortality rates, and contributing to mental health issues (177)

Floods can impact Human Health physically by increasing the risk of waterborne diseases from contaminated water supplies, and mentally by contributing to stress-related conditions such as anxiety, depression, and post-traumatic stress disorder (PTSD) (175)

Wildfires produce harmful smoke, gases, and particulate matter that can cause fatalities and affect Human Health, ranging from eye and respiratory tract irritation to more serious conditions such as lung dysfunction, bronchitis, asthma, and premature death, particularly among vulnerable groups (181)
Lack of Water can lead to poor sanitation, which increases the risk of water-borne diseases such as diarrhea, cholera, and typhoid fever (180)

Food Insecurity directly impacts Human Health by limiting access to adequate nutrition, increasing the risk of chronic diseases such as diabetes and heart disease, and contributing to mental health disorders (196)
Reduced Agricultural Yields threaten Food Security, especially in vulnerable communities, as falling yields disrupt supply chains and inflate prices (178)
Food Production Shortages can lead to Food Insecurity when they are prolonged or severe

Heatwaves can lead to Food Insecurity by disrupting food systems across Livestock Farming, Aquaculture, and Crop Farming, reducing yields, harming animal and plant health, degrading food quality, and shortening shelf life (173, 174)

Food Insecurity threatens Human Lives through acute starvation, severe malnutrition, and increased vulnerability to otherwise preventable diseases. The latest SOFI report estimates 673 million people experienced hunger in 2024, while the WFP reports 319 million people facing acute food insecurity across at least 67 countries in 2025 (197)
Reduced Agricultural Yields can trigger Economic Decline with increases in input costs, lost revenues, and localised Food Insecurity (178)

Food Production Shortages drive Economic Decline by creating supply shocks that reduce output, increase inflationary pressure, disrupt supply chains, lower purchasing power, raise labour costs, reduce profitability, and increase malnutrition (160)

Heatwaves can impact the economy by increasing living costs as demand for healthcare, energy, water, and food rises; raising infrastructure costs for operation, maintenance, repair, and replacement; and reducing labour productivity and availability due to effects on worker health and well-being (173)

Melting Glaciers and Snow can negatively impact mountain communities that depend on glacier- and snow-based tourism, resulting in significant Economic Losses and reducing the livelihoods of many people (193)
Floods affect both low and high-income countries, damaging infrastructure such as bridges, sewage systems, and power plants, resulting in significant Economic Decline (175, 176)
Wildfires and Bushfires can destroy large numbers of buildings, homes, and essential infrastructure, leading to significant Economic Losses and Decline (181)

label: Associates with
title: Excessive nitrogen and phosphorus fertilizers applied to boost yields in Corn Farming result in nutrient imbalances and runoff risks. (119)

label: Associates with
title: Intensive application of herbicides like atrazine and sulfonylureas in Corn Farming leads to persistent residues in soil, affecting crop rotation and non-target organisms. (118)

label: Associates with
title: Corn is one of the most widely grown crops globally, with about 33% of production occurring in highly water-stressed areas, raising concerns about water supply and long-term sustainability. (148)

label: Associates with
title: Tillage and monocropping in Corn Farming erode topsoil and deplete organic matter, harming soil structure. (120)

label: Contributes to
title: Rising global demand for Corn, primarily fueled by the Livestock and Ethanol industries, is driving land expansion, which often leads to Deforestation, especially in Brazil, one of the world's largest Corn Producers. (123)

label: Associates with
title: Corn Farming is often practiced as a monoculture, especially in large-scale, industrialized agriculture. ()
Cattle Farming supplies live cattle to the Cattle Slaughtering industry, where animals are processed into meat and byproducts.
Cattle Farming produces live Cattle that are traded domestically and internationally for Breeding or Slaughter.
Rendering Plants process raw byproducts from Cattle Slaughtering—including Bones, Cartilage, Hides, Fat, Offal, Blood, Hooves, and Horns—into usable materials.
Farmed Pigs are transported to Slaughtering facilities for killing and initial processing
Pig Farming produces pigs that are widely traded for Breeding, Fattening, or Slaughter.
Rendering Plants collect and process byproducts from Pig Slaughtering, such as Bones, Cartilage, Fat, Offal, and Blood, into industrial ingredients.
The Animal Rendering Industry supplies prepared Animal Skins and Hides to the Tanning Industry for leather production.

The Animal Rendering Industry supplies the Pharmaceuticals Industry with materials such as Collagen, Gelatin, Chondroitin Sulfate, Heparin, and Insulin, derived from Bovine, Porcine, and Avian byproducts.

The Rendering Industry provides the Cosmetics Industry with ingredients including Animal Fats, Gelatin, Collagen, Keratin, Glycerin (Glycerol), and Stearic Acid, used as emollients, thickeners, and active components in personal care products.

label: Causes
title: Broiler Poultry Farming faces similar risks from Highly Pathogenic Avian Influenza (HPAI) as the Layer Industry, but is relatively more resilient due to its shorter production cycles and faster turnover (157)
Poultry Farming produces birds traded domestically and internationally for Breeding, Egg Production, Fattening, or Slaughter.
Farmed Avian species are supplied to the Poultry Slaughtering industry for killing and initial carcass processing.
Crustacean Fisheries supply a major share of raw material to Crustacean Processing, contributing roughly 4–6 million tons of wild-caught crustaceans each year. (34)
Shrimp and Prawn Farming supplies around 4 million tons of farmed shrimp annually; together, farmed and wild shrimp dominate Crustacean Processing volumes. (31, 34)
Lobster Farming contributes a small but growing share of inputs to Crustacean Processing, remaining minor compared with wild catch. (36)
Crab Farming is an expanding aquaculture segment that provides a minor share of raw material for Crustacean Processing. (37)
Crayfish Farming supplies a small but growing portion of inputs to Crustacean Processing, with volumes well below Shrimp and Crab. (34)
Byproducts from Poultry Slaughtering are processed by the Rendering Industry into usable materials, though at much smaller volumes than Bovine or Porcine sources.

Spent Layer Chickens are sent to slaughterhouses for processing into low-grade Meat Products and various Byproducts for downstream industries, or may be culled on site depending on operational practices.
The Forestry Industry supplies Wood as the primary raw material for Paper Manufacturing.

label: Causes
title: The Poultry Farming and Egg Production Industry faces significant risks from the rapid spread of Highly Pathogenic Avian Influenza (HPAI), introduced by migrating wild birds and exacerbated by high-density farming, inadequate biosecurity, and viral adaptation within local wild bird populations (157)
The Egg Breaking Industry is a high-speed sector of the Poultry/Egg Production Industry that processes Eggs into liquid, frozen, or dried egg products.

label: Glucosamine: Main Ingredient in Joint Health Supplements. Health or nutritional benefits
title: Glucosamine is a primary ingredient in many Joint Health Supplements and is commonly used to support joint function and relieve joint discomfort.. Evidence: Moderate (23)

label: Chondroitin: Main Ingredient in Joint Health Supplements. Health or nutritional benefits
title: Chondroitin is frequently paired with Glucosamine in Joint Health Supplements to support cartilage health and joint comfort.. Evidence: Limited (28, 29)

label: Collagen (Type I, III): Active Ingredient in Joint Health Supplements. Health or nutritional benefits
title: Collagen is a key ingredient in Joint Health Supplements, believed to support cartilage health and improve joint flexibility. Evidence: Moderate (99, 100)

label: Marine Collagen (Type I): Active Ingredient in Joint Health Supplements. Health or nutritional benefits
title: Marine Collagen is commonly used in Joint Health Supplements for its small peptide size, which is thought to improve absorption and support cartilage health and joint mobility. Evidence: Moderate (99, 100)

label: MSM (Methylsulfonylmethane): Active Ingredient in Joint Health Supplements. Health or nutritional benefits
title: MSM (Methylsulfonylmethane) is used in Joint Health Supplements, where it is thought to support joint comfort, flexibility, and mobility.. Evidence: Moderate (109)

label: Hyaluronic Acid: Active Ingredient in Joint Health Supplements. Health or nutritional benefits
title: Hyaluronic Acid is used in Joint Health Supplements, where it is thought to support joint lubrication, cushioning, and overall mobility.. Evidence: Moderate (110)

label: Gelatin: Coating Agent in Confectionery (general). Provides Coating
title: Gelatin is commonly used in Confectionery to provide structure, chewiness, and stability.. Evidence: Not Applicable

label: Gelatin: Excipient in Capsules. Controls Release
title: Gelatin is the primary material used in Capsules, forming a protective shell for powders, liquids, or supplements.. Evidence: Not Applicable

label: Collagen (Type I, III): Main Ingredient in Collagen Peptides/Hydrolysed Collagen. Health or nutritional benefits
title: Collagen is the main ingredient in Collagen Peptides and Hydrolysed Collagen, providing short-chain proteins thought to support skin, hair, nails, and joint health. Evidence: Moderate (99, 100)

label: Marine Collagen (Type I): Main Ingredient in Collagen Peptides/Hydrolysed Collagen. Health or nutritional benefits
title: Marine Collagen is also included in Collagen Peptides and Hydrolysed Collagen products as a premium ingredient, used alongside Collagen from bovine or porcine sources. Its smaller peptide size is believed to make it more easily absorbed and support the health of skin, hair, nails, and joints.. Evidence: Moderate (99, 100)

label: Collagen (Type I, III): Active Ingredient in Collagen Face Cream. Health or nutritional benefits
title: Collagen is used in Collagen Face Creams, thought to help improve skin elasticity and hydration. Evidence: Moderate (102)

label: Marine Collagen (Type I): Active Ingredient in Collagen Face Cream. Health or nutritional benefits
title: Marine Collagen is also used in Collagen Face Creams as a higher-quality ingredient alongside Collagen from land-animal sources. Due to its smaller peptide size, it is thought to be more easily absorbed by the skin, helping enhance skin hydration and improve elasticity.. Evidence: Limited (102)

label: Gelatin: Biding Agent in Sausages. Enhances Stability
title: Gelatin is used in Sausages to improve binding, enhance juiciness, and create a firmer, more uniform texture by holding water and fat together during cooking.. Evidence: Not Applicable

label: Collagen (Type I, III): Coating Agent in Sausages. Provides Coating
title: Collagen is used in Sausages, primarily as a casing material and structural component, helping maintain shape, bite, and overall texture during processing and cooking.. Evidence: Not Applicable

label: Calcium Carbonate (CaCO3): Excipient in Tablets (Coated, Uncoated). Aids Manufacturing, Enhance Stability, Improve Drug Delivery and Patient Experience
title: Calcium Carbonate is widely used in the Pharmaceutical Industry as an inert filler, binder, and excipient in Tablets to buffer pH and support controlled drug delivery, as well as a bulking agent in Liquid Medicines, Ointments, and Creams.. Evidence: Industrially Established

label: Gelatin: Coating Agent in Tablets (Coated, Uncoated). Provides Coating
title: Gelatin is used in Coated Tablets to form a smooth, protective outer layer that improves swallowability and helps control how the tablet dissolves in the body.. Evidence: Not Applicable

label: Gelatin: Active Ingredient in Wound Dressings. Health or nutritional benefits
title: Gelatin is used in Wound Dressings to create a supportive, absorbent matrix that retains moisture, protects the wound surface, and supports the healing process.. Evidence: Not Applicable

label: Collagen (Type I, III): Active Ingredient in Wound Dressings. Health or nutritional benefits
title: Collagen is used in Wound Dressings as a bioactive material thought to support tissue regeneration, promote cell growth, and assist the body’s natural wound-healing processes.. Evidence: Not Applicable

label: Glucosamine: Active Ingredient in Pet Supplements. Health or nutritional benefits
title: Glucosamine is commonly used in Pet Supplements, where it is thought to support joint health, mobility, and comfort in dogs and other companion animals.. Evidence: Moderate (103, 105, 106)

label: Chondroitin: Active Ingredient in Pet Supplements. Health or nutritional benefits
title: Chondroitin is often included in Pet Supplements alongside Glucosamine, where it is thought to help maintain cartilage structure and support joint function.. Evidence: Limited (104, 105)

label: Collagen (Type I, III): Active Ingredient in Pet Supplements. Health or nutritional benefits
title: Collagen is used in Pet Supplements, where it is thought to support joint health, skin integrity, and coat condition by contributing to connective tissue structure.. Evidence: Moderate (106)

label: Gelatin: Gelling Agent in Energy Gels. Forms Gel Structure
title: Gelatin is used in Energy Gels as a thickening and stabilising agent, improving texture and consistency for easier consumption.. Evidence: Not Applicable

label: Collagen (Type I, III): Active Ingredient in Energy Gels. Health or nutritional benefits
title: Collagen is used in Energy Gels as a functional protein ingredient, thought to support joint health and overall connective tissue maintenance.. Evidence: Moderate (99, 100)

label: Collagen (Type I, III): Active Ingredient in Protein Bars. Health or nutritional benefits
title: Collagen is added to Protein Bars as a protein source, where it is thought to support joint health and help maintain skin, hair, and nail condition.. Evidence: Moderate (99, 100)

label: Gelatin: Biding Agent in Gummies. Adds texture or viscosity
title: Gelatin is widely used in Gummies as a binding and gelling agent, providing their chewy texture and structural stability.. Evidence: Not Applicable

label: Gelatin: Biding Agent in Photographic Film. Provides Coating
title: Gelatin is used in Photographic Film to form a light-sensitive and protective layer that holds photosensitive chemicals in place and preserves image detail.. Evidence: Not Applicable

label: Surimi: Main Ingredient in Crab Sticks. Forms Base Texture
title: Surimi is the main ingredient in Crab Sticks, providing the base protein and characteristic texture that mimics crab meat.. Evidence: Not Applicable

label: Calcium Carbonate (CaCO3): Active Ingredient in Antacids (Tums, Rolaids). Health or nutritional benefits
title: Calcium Carbonate is a long-established ingredient in OTC Antacids, widely used for its proven ability to neutralise stomach acid and provide relief from indigestion and heartburn.. Evidence: Substantial

label: Calcium Carbonate (CaCO3): Main Ingredient in Calcium Supplements. Health or nutritional benefits
title: Calcium Carbonate is widely used in Calcium Supplements to support Bone Health, with strong evidence for improving Bone Density but limited to moderate evidence for preventing or treating Osteoporosis.. Evidence: Moderate (151, 152)

label: Calcium Carbonate (CaCO3): Abrasive in Toothpaste. Abrades or strip material from a surface
title: Calcium Carbonate is a common ingredient in Toothpaste, acting as a mild, effective abrasive and polishing agent to remove plaque and surface stains. Evidence: Substantial (153)

label: Calcium Carbonate (CaCO3): Oil-Absorbent in Face Makeup. Keeps the skin matte
title: Calcium Carbonate is widely used in Cosmetics and Personal Care Products as a bulking, oil-absorbing, and opacifying agent, enhancing the texture and thickness of creams, lotions, and powders while providing a matte finish.. Evidence: Industrially Established

label: Calcium Carbonate (CaCO3): Odor Absorbent in Deodorants & Antiperspirants. Adsorbs moisture and odor
title: Calcium Carbonate is a safe, effective absorbent for moisture and odor in eco-conscious, talc-free, and aluminium-free formulas. Evidence: Industrially Established

label: Calcium Carbonate (CaCO3): Active Ingredient in Phosphate Binders. Health or nutritional benefits
title: Calcium Carbonate is a commonly used, cost-effective phosphate binder for managing Hyperphosphatemia in Chronic Kidney Disease patients, binding dietary phosphorus in the gut to reduce absorption. It is effective but carries a risk of Hypercalcemia.. Evidence: Clinically Nuanced (154, 155)

label: Calcium Carbonate (CaCO3): Filler in Bar Soap. Adds volume, structure, bulk, texture, or lubrication
title: Calcium Carbonate is widely used in Detergents as a cost-effective, environmentally friendly bulking agent in powder and cream formulations, enhancing the detergency-building action of Sodium Carbonate.. Evidence: Industrially Established

label: Calcium Carbonate (CaCO3): Filler in Detergents. Adds volume, structure, bulk, texture, or lubrication
title: Calcium Carbonate is widely used in Detergents as a cost-effective, environmentally friendly bulking agent in powder and cream formulations, improving flowability, enhancing the detergency-building action of Sodium Carbonate, and supporting better storage stability. Evidence: Industrially Established

label: Gelatin: Main Ingredient in Jello. Forms Gel Structure
title: Gelatin is the key ingredient in Jello, responsible for its gel-like texture and structure.. Evidence: Not Applicable

label: Calcium Carbonate (CaCO3): Functional Filler in Lotions. Imparts specific properties beyond just reducing costs
title: Calcium Carbonate can be added to Lotions as an opacifying and mattifying agent, as well as a texture enhancer and oil absorbent. Evidence: Industrially Established

label: Calcium Carbonate (CaCO3): Nutritional Fortifier in Commercial Bread. Health or nutritional benefits
title: Calcium Carbonate is commonly added to Commercial Bread as a dough conditioner, yeast nutrient, and pH regulator to improve texture, volume, and yeast activity. Evidence: Not Applicable (156)

label: Calcium Carbonate (CaCO3): Nutritional Fortifier in Fortified Bread Flour. Health or nutritional benefits
title: Calcium Carbonate is voluntarily added to Flour in many regions and is a mandatory fortificant in the United Kingdom for non-wholemeal flour to enhance nutritional value. Evidence: Not Applicable

label: Calcium Carbonate (CaCO3): pH Regulator in Biscuits & Cookies. Stabilize pH of a solution or product
title: Calcium Carbonate is a common additive in Biscuits and Cookies as a nutritional fortificant and functional dough conditioner, helping to regulate pH, control consistency, and improve texture. Evidence: Industrially Established

label: Calcium Carbonate (CaCO3): Additive in Pastries. Conditioner, leavening agent, pH regulator
title: Calcium Carbonate is a common additive in the Baking Industry, used in Pastries as a dough conditioner, leavening aid, pH regulator, and nutrient fortifier due to its affordability and efficiency. Evidence: Industrially Established

label: Calcium Carbonate (CaCO3): Additive in Cakes. Conditioner, leavening agent, pH regulator
title: Calcium Carbonate can be used in Cakes as an additive for leavening, dough conditioning, texture enhancement, pH regulation, and anti-caking. Evidence: Industrially Established

label: Calcium Carbonate (CaCO3): Additive in Quick Breads. Conditioner, leavening agent, pH regulator
title: Calcium Carbonate can be added to commercial Quick Breads such as Pancakes and Waffles as a dough conditioner, pH regulator, fortification agent, and leavening agent. Evidence: Industrially Established

label: Calcium Carbonate (CaCO3): Nutritional Fortifier in Breakfast/Cereal/Snack Bars. Health or nutritional benefits
title: Calcium Carbonate is a common food additive used in Breakfast/Cereal/Snack Bars for Calcium fortification to enhance nutritional value. Evidence: Not Applicable (156)

label: Gelatin: Biding Agent in Marshmallows. Adds texture or viscosity
title: Gelatin is a key ingredient in Marshmallows, giving them their soft, spongy texture.. Evidence: Not Applicable

label: Gelatin: Thickener in Dairy Yogurt. Adds texture or viscosity
title: Gelatin is sometimes added to Dairy Yogurt to improve thickness and create a smooth, creamy texture.. Evidence: Not Applicable

label: Gelatin: Stabiliser in Cream Cheese. Enhances Stability
title: Gelatin is used in Cream Cheese to help stabilise texture and improve firmness.. Evidence: Not Applicable

label: Gelatin: Stabiliser in Sour Cream. Enhances Stability
title: Gelatin is added to Sour Cream to enhance thickness and maintain a consistent texture.. Evidence: Not Applicable

label: Gelatin: Emulsifier in Margarine. Improves Spreadability
title: Gelatin is occasionally used in Margarine to help stabilise texture and improve spreadability.. Evidence: Not Applicable

label: Gelatin: Stabiliser in Ice Cream. Prevents Ice Crystal Formation
title: Gelatin is often added to Ice Cream to improve creaminess and maintain a smooth, stable texture.. Evidence: Not Applicable

label: Crustacean Shells is a Byproduct of Crustacean Processing
title: The Crustacean Processing industry is the primary source of shells, particularly from shrimp, crabs, and lobsters, processing 6-8 million tons annually, 50-70% of crustacean weight are discarded as shells, cephalothoraxes, and exoskeletons (30, 31, 34)

label: Crustacean Shells is a Byproduct of Crustacean Fisheries
title: Crustacean Fisheries are a major source of shells, especially from wild-caught shrimp, crabs, and lobsters (48)

label: Crustacean Shells is a Byproduct of Shrimp and Prawn Farming
title: Shrimp and Prawn Farming contribute significantly to shell supply due to high production (40, 41)

label: Crustacean Shells is a Byproduct of Lobster Farming
title: Lobster Farming is a minor shell source compared to other crustaceans (36)

label: Crustacean Shells is a Byproduct of Crab Farming
title: Crab Farming contributes moderately to shell supply, though volumes are lower than shrimp or prawn (44)

label: Crustacean Shells is a Byproduct of Crayfish Farming
title: Crayfish Farming is a minor contributor to shell supply at lower volumes than shrimp and crab (47)

label: Wild-Caught Fish is a Target Catch of Commercial Fishing
title: Wild-Caught Fish are harvested through Commercial Fishing and Artisanal Fishing primarily for human consumption, common species including Cod, Tuna, Anchovy, Herring, and Mackerel

label: Wild-Caught Fish is a Target Catch of Artisanal Fishing
title: Yields of WIld-Caught Fish from Artisanal Fishing are much smaller than Commercial Fishing, primarily for local consumption, catches are less likely to be processed into byproducts

label: Eggs is a Main Product of Layer Poultry Farming/Egg Production
title: Egg Production is a high-volume global sector centred on the breeding and farming of Layer Poultry, predominantly Chickens.

label: Eggs is a Raw Material of Egg Breaking Industry
title: The Egg Breaking Industry is a high-speed sector of the Poultry and Egg Production Industry that processes Eggs into liquid, frozen, or dried egg products and a primary source for Egg Shells as byproduct.

label: Direct product of
title: Laying Hens, primarily White Leghorn in commercial Egg Production, can produce around 290 to 300 Eggs per year.

label: Layer Poultry is a Source of Pet Food Industry
title: Spent Layer Chicken Meat and byproducts such as Fats are commonly used in Pet Food and other lower-grade processed products.

label: Layer Poultry is a Primary Product of Layer Poultry Farming/Egg Production
title: The Layer Chicken Farming Industry breeds and raises specialised Hens for high-volume Egg Production rather than Meat, with each Hen capable of laying up to around 300 Eggs per year.

label: Filamentuous Fungi is a Main Product of Biotechnology
title: Filamentous Fungi is cultivated at industrial-scale in the Biotechnology industry by selecting or engineering suitable fungal strains in lab-controlled environments

label: Bovine (Cow) is a Primary Product of Cattle Farming
title: Bovine refers to domesticated Cattle raised in the Cattle Farming Industry for meat, milk, breeding, and other purposes

label: Bovine (Cow) is a Raw Material of Cattle Slaughtering
title: The Cattle Slaughtering Industry processes Bovines for meat as the primary product before further processing in downstream industries

label: Bovine (Cow) is a Raw Material of Tanning Industry
title: Bovine is the primary raw material for the Leather Processing industry, whose products include leather, gelatin, collagen, and related materials

label: Bovine (Cow) is a Raw Material of Livestock Trading (Bovine)
title: Live Bovines are traded in the Livestock Trade industry for breeding, fattening, or slaughter

label: Bovine (Cow) is a Raw Material of Rendering (Animals)
title: Non-meat parts of Bovine are sent to the Rendering Industry for processing

label: Bovine (Cow) is a Source of Pharmaceuticals Industry
title: Bovine byproducts are an important source for the Pharmaceutical Industry, used in the production of Heparin, Insulin, Collagen, Gelatin, and other medical materials

label: Bovine (Cow) is a Source of Pet Food Industry
title: Bovine products, byproducts, and derivatives—such as meat trimmings, organs, fat, bone meal, and tallow—are used in the Pet Food Industry as sources of protein, nutrients, and energy

label: Bovine (Cow) is a Source of Cosmetics Industry
title: Bovine byproducts and derivatives—such as Tallow, Collagen, Gelatin, Keratin, and Glycerin—are widely used in the Cosmetics Industry as emollients, stabilisers, and active ingredients in skincare and personal care products

label: Sharks and Elasmobranchs is a Bycatch of Fisheries
title: Sharks and Elasmobranchs (e.g. rays, skates) are unintentionally caught alongside target species during commercial or artisanal fishing operations

label: Sharks and Elasmobranchs is a Target Catch of Shark Fisheries
title: Sharks and Elasmobranchs (e.g. rays, skates) are deliberately caught in targeted fisheries for their meat, fins, cartilage, or other parts

label: Porcine (Pig) is a Primary Product of Pig Farming
title: Porcine refers to domesticated Pigs raised in the Pig Farming Industry for meat production, breeding, and related uses

label: Porcine (Pig) is a Primary Product of Pig Slaughtering
title: The Pig Slaughtering Industry processes Pigs for meat as the primary product before further processing in downstream industries

label: Porcine (Pig) is a Primary Product of Livestock Trading (Porcine)
title: Live Porcine Animals are traded across markets for breeding, fattening, or slaughter

label: Porcine (Pig) is a Raw Material of Rendering (Animals)
title: Non-meat parts from Porcine are sent to the Rendering Industry for processing

label: Porcine (Pig) is a Source of Pharmaceuticals Industry
title: Porcine byproducts—such as intestinal mucosa, glands, and tissues—are key sources for the Pharmaceutical Industry, used in the production of Heparin, Insulin, Gelatin, and other medical products

label: Porcine (Pig) is a Source of Pet Food Industry
title: Porcine products, byproducts, and derivatives such as meat trimmings, organs, fat, and rendered meals are widely used in the Pet Food industry as essential protein, fat, and nutrient sources

label: Porcine (Pig) is a Source of Cosmetics Industry
title: Porcine byproducts and derivatives—such as Collagen, Gelatin, Fat, and Glycerin—are used in the Cosmetics Industry for their moisturizing, texturizing, and stabilising properties in skincare and personal care products

label: Broiler Avian (Poultry/Fowls) is a Raw Material of Rendering (Animals)
title: Avian slaughter byproducts are sent to the Rendering industry but in much smaller volumes than Bovine and Porcine, contributing minimally to downstream industries like Pharmaceuticals and Cosmetics

label: Broiler Avian (Poultry/Fowls) is a Source of Pet Food Industry
title: Avian meat, byproducts, and derivatives are used in the Pet Food industry as protein and nutrient sources

label: Broiler Avian (Poultry/Fowls) is a Primary Product of Poultry Farming (Broilers)
title: Avian species like chickens, ducks, and turkeys are bred and raised in the Poultry Farming industry for meat, eggs, and other products

label: Broiler Avian (Poultry/Fowls) is a Primary Product of Livestock Trading (Poultry)
title: Avian species are traded internationally as livestock for breeding or production purposes

label: Broiler Avian (Poultry/Fowls) is a Primary Product of Poultry Slaughtering
title: Avian species are sent to the Slaughtering industry for meat production as the primary output

label: Farmed Fish is a Primary Product of Fish Aquaculture
title: Fish Aquaculture produces Farmed Fish primarily for human consumption, commonly Carp, Tilapia, Catfish, Salmon, and Pangasius

label: Dairy Cows is a Primary Product of Dairy Farming
title: Dairy cows are raised in Dairy Farming to produce milk and related dairy products

label: Lignin (Wood) is a Byproduct of Paper Manufacturing
title: Lignin is extracted as a byproduct during wood processing in the forestry industry, primarily from pulp and paper manufacturing

label: Global Leader
title: The USA is the global leader in filamentous fungal research and biotech, with focus on enzymes, pharmaceuticals, and agriculture (60)

label: Top Producer & Significant Exporter
title: The US is the largest global Bovine Producer (Beef) (~12.89M tonnes, 2022), generating various co-products, by-products, and sub-products from bovine processing (48, 49, 50). The US is one of the top global Bovine/Beef Exporters with ~1.2M-1.5M tonnes (2024-2025) (48, 49, 51, 52)

label: Significant Producer & Significant Exporter
title: The US is a major Porcine (Pork) Producer after China and the EU, ~12.8M tonnes, ~11% of global production (48, 53, 54). The US is likely the second largest Porcine (Pork) Exporter globally, ~3.03M tonnes, 2024 (48, 55, 71)

label: Top Producer & Top Exporter
title: The US is the world's largest Poultry Producer, ~22.5M metric tonnes, ~20% global output, 2024 (48, 57). The US is the second-largest Poultry Exporter, close behind Brazil, ~2.9-3.5M tonnes (57, 58, 59)

label: Top Consumer
title: The US is a significant consumer of Chitin, Crustacean Shells (raw material), and derivatives—especially in pharmaceuticals and biodegradable materials—likely second only to China (7, 48)

label: Top Importer
title: The US is the top Importer of fish waste byproducts, including Fish Scales and Skins, valued at $113.59M in 2024 (111)

label: Top Importer
title: The US is the top Importer of fish waste byproducts, including Fish Scales and Skins, valued at $113.59M in 2024 (111)

label: Top Importer & Recognised Consumer
title: The US is the top Importer of Fish and other fishery products with import value ~$26.6B in 2024 (112). The US is a recognised Consumer of Fish and seafood, though far behind China, India, and Indonesia by volume, with ~7.5M tonnes in 2022 (114)

label: Top Consumer
title: USA is the largest consumer of Corn and corn products (e.g. ethanol, livestock feed, food, and industrial products) (14)

label: Top Consumer & Significant Importer
title: The USA is top Beef Consumer, both in per capita and total consumption (~12.7M tonnes, 2025) (48, 49). The US is a major Importer of Beef for processed foods (~2M tonnes, 2025), despite being a leading producer and exporter (48, 49)

label: Significant Consumer
title: The US is a significant Consumer of Pork with high total volumes, ~27kg/yr per capita (48, 53)

label: Top Consumer
title: The US leads in Poultry Consumption, with the highest total and per capita intake, ~17.9M tonnes, 2023 (57)

label: Major Source
title: Indonesia produces significant quantities of Crustacean Shells, notably from Shrimp and Crab, derived from both aquaculture and wild-catch fisheries (17, 18, 81)

label: Significant Catcher
title: Indonesia is the 2nd largest Catcher of Wild-Caught Fish, though far behind China, with ~7M tonnes caught in 2020 (116)

label: Significant Producer
title: Indonesia is the 2nd largest Producer of Farmed Fish after China, though at a much lower volume, with ~16.7 M tonnes in 2021 (113, 115)

label: Significant Consumer
title: Indonesia is among the top 3 Consumers of Fish and seafood, close to India, with ~11.4 M tonnes in 2022 (114)

label: Top Player
title: The EU has an established filamentous fungal research hub, focusing on industrial enzymes, pharmaceuticals, and environmental biotech (60)

label: Recognised Producer
title: The EU is a significant Bovine/Beef Producer, mainly for domestic consumption (~6.5M tonnes, 2025) (49)

label: Significant Producer & Top Exporter
title: The EU is the second largest Porcine (Pork) Producer, ~21M tonnes, ~18.3% of global production (48, 53, 54). The EU is the largest global Porcine (Pork) Exporter, ~30-31% of world pork exports, ~10.4M tonnes, 2024 (48, 53)

label: Recognised Producer & Significant Exporter
title: The EU is an emerging Producer of Poultry, ~12.0M metric tonnes, ~11.3% of global production (48, 57). The EU is a significant Poultry Exporter, with Poland, the Netherlands, and Spain as top contributors, ~1.8-2.5M tonnes (57, 58, 59)

label: Significant Importer
title: The EU is a major Importer of fish waste byproducts by quantity, including Fish Scales and Skins, ~199,651,000 kg, 2024 (111)

label: Significant Importer
title: The EU is a major Importer of fish waste byproducts by quantity, including Other Fish Parts, ~199,651,000 kg, 2024 (111)

label: Top Importer
title: The EU is the leading Importer of Fish and other fisheries and aquaculture products as a whole (although none of its member states has import values close to the top three), accounting for ~35% of total world fish imports by value (112)

label: Recognised Consumer
title: The EU is a significant Beef Consumer with substantial domestic demand across member countries (~6.2M tonnes, 2025) (49)

label: Top Consumer
title: The EU ranks among the top Pork Consumers, ~20.5M tonnes (48, 53)

label: Significant Consumer & Significant Importer
title: The EU is a major Poultry Consumer, with steady demand and strong domestic consumption (57). The EU is a notable Poultry Importer alongside China, with imports ~0.7M tonnes (57)

label: Recognised Exporter
title: Ecuador is a recognised Exporter of Farmed Fish, though well behind China and Norway in value, with ~$9.2 B in 2024 (112)

label: Top Player
title: Brazil is an emerging player in filamentous fungal science, leveraging fungal bioactive compounds and sustainable bioprocessing (60)

label: Top Producer & Top Exporter
title: Brazil is the world's 2nd largest Bovine Producer (Beef) (~10.35M tonnes, 2022), generating abundant co-products, by-products, and sub-products (48, 49, 50). Brazil is the world's largest Bovine/Beef Exporter by volume ~2.9M-3.6M tonnes (2024-2025) (48, 49, 51, 52)

label: Recognised Producer & Recognised Exporter
title: Brazil is among the top five global Porcine (Pork) Producers, ~4.5-4.8M tonnes (48, 53, 54). Brazil is an emerging Porcine (Pork) Exporter and a competitive supplier to Asia (48, 53, 55)

label: Significant Producer & Top Exporter
title: Brazil is a major Poultry Producer, close to China, export-oriented, ~15M metric tonnes, ~15.1% global production (48, 57). Brazil ranks as the top global Exporter of Poultry, ~4-5M metric tonnes (57, 58, 59)

label: Significant Consumer
title: Brazil is a major consumer of Corn alongside being a top producer (15)

label: Top Player
title: Brazil has the third largest market share of Shark Cartilage product, ~$14.13M sales revenue (2025) (66)

label: Significant Consumer
title: Brazil is a notable Beef Consumer, with high domestic consumption (~8.2M tonnes, 2025) (48, 49)

label: Significant Consumer
title: Brazil is a major Poultry Consumer, with strong domestic demand and high per capita intake due to its affordability, ~10.1M tonnes (57)

label: Recognised Producer & Significant Exporter
title: Argentina is a well-known traditional Bovine Producer (Beef) (~3.14M tonnes, 2022), popular for its grass-fed cattle and high-quality beef (48, 49, 50). Argentina is a notable Exporter of premium grass-fed Beef, with ~600–860K tonnes exported (2024–2025), though behind top producers in volume (48, 49, 51, 52)

label: Significant Importer
title: South Korea is one of the major importers of corn, for feed and industrial use (12)

label: Recognised Importer
title: South Korea is an emerging Beef Importer (~575K tonnes, 2025), particularly premium beef from the US and Australia (48, 49)

label: Recognised Producer
title: Bangladesh is a recognised Producer of Farmed Fish, alongside Vietnam, ranking after the top three (China, Indonesia, India), with ~6.3M tonnes in 2021 (113, 115)

label: Significant Exporter
title: Australia is the 2nd largest Bovine meat (Beef) Exporter in the world, exporting ~1.69M-1.9M tonnes (2024-2025) (48, 49, 51, 52)

label: Top Importer
title: The UK is a top-three global Poultry Importer, importing ~0.94M tonnes (48, 57)

label: Recognised Importer
title: Spain is a notable EU Importer of Fish, valued at ~$9.4B in 2024 (112)

label: Recognised Importer
title: France is a notable EU Importer of Fish after Spain and Italy, with value ~$7.7B in 2024 (112)

label: Recognised Exporter
title: Canada is an emerging Exporter of Porcine (Pork), primarily to the US and Asia (48, 53, 55)

label: Recognised Importer
title: Canada is a significant Importer of fish waste byproducts, including Fish Scales and Skins, valued at ~$80.5M, with a total import quantity of ~43.78M kg, 2024 (111)

label: Recognised Importer
title: Canada is a significant Importer of fish waste byproducts, including Other Fish Parts, valued at ~$80.5M, with a total import quantity of ~43.78M kg, 2024 (111)

label: Top Player
title: Canada has the second largest market share of Shark Cartilage product, ~$17.871M sales revenue (2025) (66)

label: Recognised Catcher
title: Peru is a recognised Catcher of Wild-Caught Fish, with >5.5M tonnes caught in 2020 (116)

label: Recognised Catcher
title: Russia is a recognised Catcher of Wild-Caught Fish closely behind Peru and India, with ~5M tonnes in 2020 (116)

label: Significant Player
title: Germany leads Europe's Shark Cartilage product market, generating ~$7.19M in sales revenue (2025) (66)

label: Significant Player
title: Nigeria leads Africa in Shark Cartilage product sales, with ~$6.435M in revenue (2025) (66)

label: Recognised Importer
title: Italy is a notable EU Importer of Fish alongside Spain and France, valued at ~8B in 2024 (112)

label: Recognised Importer
title: Denmark is a significant Importer of fish waste byproducts, including Fish Scales and Skins, valued at ~$79.3M, with a total import quantity of ~235.54M kg, 2024 (111)

label: Recognised Importer
title: Denmark is a significant Importer of fish waste byproducts, including Other Fish Parts, valued at ~$79.3M, with a total import quantity of ~235.54M kg, 2024 (111)

label: Top Exporter & Top Producer
title: China is the top global Exporter of Crustacean Shells (shrimp, crab, etc.) and derivatives (e.g. Chitin), driven by abundant supply and low-cost production (6, 17, 79, 80). China can be considered as the top Crustacean Shells Producer and Chitin Producer (~80% of global supply) for its abundant shell supply, driven by extensive shrimp and crab aquaculture industries (6, 17, 79, 80)

label: Top Catcher & Global Leader
title: China is the leading global Wild Fish Catcher, with over 13 M tonnes captured in 2020 (116). China is leading as the largest Exporter of Wild-Caught Fish and seafood in general (117)

label: Top Player
title: China is a key player in filamentous fungi production, with a rapidly growing industry focused on fermentation, enzyme production, and bioremediation (60)

label: Significant Producer
title: China ranks 3rd in Bovine Production (Beef) after the US and Brazil, producing ~7.18M tonnes in 2022, mainly for domestic consumption (48, 49, 50)

label: Top Producer
title:
China is the world's largest Porcine (Pork) Producer, ~55-56M tonnes, accounts for >50% of the global pig population
  (48, 53, 54)

label: Significant Producer & Recognised Exporter
title: China has significant Poultry Production, ~17-20M metric tonnes, ~16.6% of global production (48, 57). China is rapidly expanding Poultry Exports, particularly in processed chicken, ~0.8-1M tonnes (57, 58, 59)

label: Top Producer & Top Exporter
title: China is the world's largest Fish Producer, ~67.8M tonnes, 2024 (113). China is the largest Exporter of Farmed Fish in the world, with a value of ~$20.18 B in 2024 (112)

label: Top Importer & Top Consumer
title: China is amongst the top 3 importers of Chitin, while also being the largest producer and exporter (6). China is a leading consumer market for Chitin, driven by the increasing demands in agriculture, water treatment, and pharmaceuticals (48, 68)

label: Top Importer & Top Consumer
title: China is the 2nd largest Importer and reporter of Fish and other fishery products with import value ~$22.3B in 2024 (112). China is the largest Consumer of Fish and seafood, with ~59.3M tonnes in 2022 (114)

label: Significant Importer & Significant Consumer
title: China is a significant importer of Corn despite being a top producer itself due to high domestic demands, mainly for livestock feed and biofuels (11). China is the second largest consumer of Corn after the US; consumption often exceeds production, driving import demand (15)

label: Global Leader
title: China has the largest Shark Cartilage product market share, sales revenue ~$22.324M (2025) (66)

label: Top Consumer & Top Importer
title: China is closely behind the US in Beef Consumption (~11.6M tonnes, 2025), with demand continuing to rise (48, 49). China is the largest Beef Importer despite having a large bovine farming industry (~3.8M tonnes, 2025), driven by high domestic demand (48, 49)

label: Significant Importer & Top Consumer
title: China is one of the top Pork Importers due to strong domestic demand, despite being the world's largest producer (48, 53). China is the world's largest Pork Consumer, accounting for nearly half of global consumption (48, 53)

label: Top Consumer & Significant Importer
title: China is the second-largest Poultry Consumer after the US, with the fastest growth in consumption, ~ 15M tonnes, 2023 (57). China is a notable Poultry Importer after Japan, Mexico, and the UK, with imports of ~0.7M tonnes (48, 57)

label: Significant Exporter
title: Norway is the 2nd largest Exporter of aquatic animals (mostly farmed salmon), but also including Wild-Caught Fish such as cod, herring, mackerel, and some other whitefish and small pelagic species (117)

label: Significant Exporter
title: Norway is the second largest Exporter of Farmed Fish after China, primarily cultured Atlantic Salmon, with a value of ~$16 B in 2024 (112)

label: Recognised Exporter
title: Chile is a recognised Exporter of Farmed Fish, behind China, Norway, Ecuador, and Vietnam, with a value of ~$7.8 B in 2024 (112)

label: Top Importer & Significant Consumer
title: Mexico is the largest importer of Corn, driven by food and feed demand, imports mostly from the U.S. (8). Mexico is a large consumer of Corn, primarily for food and feed, substantial portion of supply imported primarily from the U.S. (16)

label: Significant Player
title: Mexico has significant market share of Shark Cartilage product, ~$9.241M sales revenue (2025) (66)

label: Significant Importer
title: Mexico is a fast-growing Pork Importer, driven by strong domestic demand (48, 53)

label: Top Importer
title: Mexico is close to Japan as a top Poultry Importer, with imports of ~0.8 to 1.01M tonnes (48, 57)

label: Significant Consumer & Significant Importer
title: Japan has high Chitin consumption primarily for biomedical applications (48, 68). Japan is a major importer of Chitin for uses in biomedical and agricultural applications (6)

label: Significant Importer & Recognised Consumer
title: Japan is the 3rd largest Importer of Fish after the US and China, valued at ~$12.9 B in 2024 (112). Japan is a recognised Consumer of Fish and seafood, behind China, India, Indonesia, and the US, with ~5.6 M tonnes in 2022 (114)

label: Significant Importer
title: Japan is a major Corn importer, mainly for animal feed (8)

label: Recognised Importer
title: Japan is a significant Beef Importer (~720K tonnes, 2025) with high demand due to strong consumption and limited local production (48, 49)

label: Top Importer
title: Japan is the world’s largest Importer of Pork (48, 53, 56)

label: Top Importer
title: Japan leads global Poultry Imports, totaling around 0.9–1.08M tonnes (48, 57)

label: Major Source & Significant Exporter
title: India is an emerging seafood Producer and Shell product supplier—incl. Chitin—with growing applications in agriculture and cosmetics (7, 17, 19, 79, 81). India is a notable Chitin Exporter, a compound derived directly from Crustacean Shells, likely ranking 2nd globally (6, 7)

label: Recognised Catcher
title: India is a recognised Catcher of Wild-Caught Fish alongside Peru, with ~5.5M tonnes in 2020 (116)

label: Recognised Producer & Significant Exporter
title: India is an emerging Bovine Producer (Beef), ~4.35M tonnes (2022), mainly of buffalo due to religious restrictions, generating various co- and by-products (48, 49, 50). India ranks among the top 5 Bovine Meat/Carabeef Exporters, mainly Buffalo meat, with ~1.4–1.6M tonnes (2024–2025) (48, 49, 51, 52)

label: Significant Producer
title: India is among the top three Producers of Farmed Fish after China and Indonesia, with ~10.9 M tonnes in 2021 (113, 115)

label: Significant Consumer
title: India is the 2nd largest Consumer of Fish and seafood, with ~12.6M tonnes in 2022 (114)

label: Significant Player
title: India has significant market share of Shark Cartilage product, ~$9.489M sales revenue (2025) (66)

label: Major Source
title: Thailand is one of the top Shrimp and prawn producers-incl. processed products, enabling abundant Crustacean Shell availability (17, 81)

label: Recognised Exporter
title: Thailand is an emerging Exporter of Poultry, supplying high-quality products to Asian markets, ~1.1-1.5M tonnes (57, 58, 59)

label: Significant Producer & Top Exporter
title: Vietnam is a significant Producer of Crustacean Shells (mainly Shrimp) and their derivative, Chitin, supported by its major Shrimp and Prawn Farming industry (6, 17, 81). Vietnam is one of the top Exporters of Chitin, derived from Crustacean Shells, primarily supplying regional markets in Asia (6, 48)

label: Recognised Exporter
title: Vietnam is a recognised Exporter of Wild-Caught Fish (Tuna), and the 3rd largest Exporter of aquatic animal products (incl. farmed fish and shrimps) (117)

label: Recognised Producer & Recognised Exporter
title: Vietnam is a recognised Producer of Farmed Fish, alongside Bangladesh, ranking after the top three (China, Indonesia, India), with ~6.4 M tonnes in 2021 (113, 115). Vietnam is a recognised Exporter of Farmed Fish, behind China, Norway, and Ecuador, with a value of ~$8.3 B in 2024 (112)

label: Significant Importer
title: Vietnam imports a significant quantity of Chitin despite being a notable producer to meet domestic demands (6)

label: Significant Importer
title: Vietnam is a notable importer of Corn, driven by feed and food processing (13)

label: Chitin can be Extracted from Crustacean Shells
title: Chitin is commonly extracted from Crustacean Shells—such as those of shrimp, crabs, and lobsters—through demineralisation (removal of calcium carbonate) and deproteinisation, leaving behind the chitin-rich structural material. (32, 33)

label: Chitin can be Extracted from Filamentuous Fungi
title: Chitin can also be extracted from the cell walls of Filamentous Fungi, offering a non-animal alternative source that is increasingly used in industrial, biomedical, and dietary applications. (35)

label: Bovine Hides can be Harvested from Bovine (Cow)
title: Bovine Hides are obtained from Cows/Cattle and are a primary byproduct of the Beef and Dairy industries.

label: Bovine Connective Tissues (Tendons, Ligaments, and Fascia) can be Harvested from Bovine (Cow)
title: Bovine Connective Tissues are harvested from Cattle during Slaughter and Meat Processing as high-collagen byproducts.

label: Fish Scales & Skin can be Harvested from Wild-Caught Fish
title: Fish Skins & Scales from Wild-Caught Fish (often species like Cod, Tilapia, and Sardines), are collected as Byproducts for further processing

label: Fish Scales & Skin can be Harvested from Farmed Fish
title: Fish Skin and Scales (esp. from tilapia, cod, carp) are lightweight and high in Type I Collagen. They’re processed into Marine Collagen, Biofilms, Wound Dressings, and cosmetic actives

label: Fish Byproducts (heads, bones, fins, vertebrae, cartilage) can be Harvested from Wild-Caught Fish
title: Other Fish Parts from Wild-Caught Fish, such as heads, bones, and viscera, are used as Byproducts for Fish Meal, Fish Oil, and Nutritional Supplments

label: Fish Byproducts (heads, bones, fins, vertebrae, cartilage) can be Harvested from Farmed Fish
title: Includes Fish Heads, Fins, and Vertebrae, rich in Collagen, minerals, and oils. Often used for Broth, Fish Meal, hydrolyzed protein, and pharmaceutical inputs

label: DMS (Dimethyl Sulfide) can be Degraded/Broken Down from Lignin (Wood)
title: DMS is formed through the chemical degradation of sulfur-containing compounds in Lignin, commonly occurring during the Kraft pulping of wood in paper production

label: DMS is a precursor of DMSO
title: DMSO is produced by chemically oxidizing DMS, a sulfur-containing compound.

label: Rooster Combs can be Harvested from Broiler Avian (Poultry/Fowls)
title: Rooster Combs can be harvested from farmed Poultry

label: Bacteria (Streptococcus spp. or Bacillus spp.) is a Primary Product of Biotechnology
title: In modern HA production, starter cultures of bacteria are maintained in biorepositories

label: Fish Meat & Products can be Harvested from Wild-Caught Fish
title: Fish Meat from Wild Caught Fish is harvested from natural marine and freshwater environments, commonly sourced from species such as Cod, Tuna, Anchovies, and Sardines

label: Fish Meat & Products can be Harvested from Farmed Fish
title: Fish Meat from Farmed Fish is sourced from aquaculture, commonly including species such as Salmon, Tilapia, Catfish, and Carp

label: Porcine Bones can be Harvested from Porcine (Pig)
title: Porcine Bones originate from farmed Pigs (Porcine) and are collected during slaughter and meat processing.

label: Corn is a Main Product of Corn Farming
title: Corn is harvested directly from the maize plant in Corn Farming

label: Avian (Poultry) Bones can be Harvested from Layer Poultry
title: Bones and other slaughter byproducts from spent Layer Hens are processed similarly to those from Broiler Chickens.

label: Avian (Poultry) Bones can be Harvested from Broiler Avian (Poultry/Fowls)
title: Avian Bones come from farmed Avian Species such as chickens, ducks, and turkeys, mainly as poultry processing byproducts.

label: Avian Skin can be Harvested from Layer Poultry
title: Skins from spent Layer Hens are widely utilised in downstream processing, particularly for Collagen and Gelatin production, as well as rendered Fats used in food, feed, and industrial application.

label: Avian Skin can be Harvested from Broiler Avian (Poultry/Fowls)
title: Avian Skins are sourced from farmed Poultry such as Chickens and Ducks during Meat Processing.

label: Pig Skin can be Harvested from Porcine (Pig)
title: Pig Skin is sourced from farmed Pigs, widely utilised in Food Processing and Ingredient Manufacturing.

label: Porcine Connective Tissues (Tendons, Ligaments, and Fascia) can be Harvested from Porcine (Pig)
title: Porcine Connective Tissues are obtained from Pigs and collected as part of standard Meat-Processing byproduct streams.

label: Poultry Connective Tissues (Tendons, Ligaments, and Fascia) can be Harvested from Layer Poultry
title: Connective Tissues from spent Layer Hens serve as sources for Collagen and Elastin production, supporting applications in Supplements, Food Ingredients, and Biomaterials.

label: Poultry Connective Tissues (Tendons, Ligaments, and Fascia) can be Harvested from Broiler Avian (Poultry/Fowls)
title: Avian Connective Tissues from Poultry are collected as bypoducts in the Poultry Industry for further uses, but with smaller quantities compared to Bovine and Porcine sources.

label: Eggshell Membrane can be Harvested from Eggs
title: Eggshell Membrane is linked to Laying Hens and the Egg Production Industry, sourced from Eggshells as a byproduct of the Egg Breaking Industry.

label: Eggshells can be Harvested from Eggs
title: Eggshells are separated from Eggs through high-speed industrial processing, then treated to remove the membrane before further use in downstream applications.

label: Limestone is a Raw Material of Quarrying
title: Limestone is primarily extracted through open-pit quarrying, involving geological surveying, removal of overburden, and rock extraction using drilling and controlled blasting

label: Limestone can be Accumulated from Shells and Skeletal Remains of Marine Organisms
title: Limestone forms mainly in marine environments through the accumulation, breakdown, and compression of Calcium Carbonate-rich materials such as Shells, Corals, and the Skeletal Remains of Marine Organisms

label: Marble is a form of Limestone
title: Marble forms through a metamorphic process in which Limestone is subjected to intense heat and pressure, typically at tectonic plate boundaries, a transformation that can take over 100 million years

label: Chalk is a Raw Material of Quarrying
title: Chalk deposits are typically near the surface and are extracted mainly through open-pit Quarrying using heavy machinery, with controlled blasting sometimes used to loosen and remove the rock

label: Chalk can be Accumulated from Shells and Skeletal Remains of Marine Organisms
title: Chalk forms over millions of years from the accumulation of microscopic Skeletal Remains of Marine Plankton, primarily Coccolithophores, and other tiny marine organisms settling on the ocean floor

label: Marble is a Raw Material of Quarrying
title: Marble is typically extracted from large stone quarries in mountainous regions using specialised equipment and controlled cutting techniques to preserve large, high-quality stone blocks

label: Marble can be Accumulated from Shells and Skeletal Remains of Marine Organisms
title: Marble originates from Shells and Skeletal Remains of Marine Organisms that first form Limestone, which is then transformed into Marble under intense heat and pressure

label: Animal Cartilage can be Harvested from Bovine (Cow)
title: Bovine animals are a major commercial source of Animal Cartilage, typically collected from the trachea, ribs, or sternum of cows and widely used for Collagen, Gelatin, and Chondroitin extraction.

label: Animal Cartilage can be Harvested from Porcine (Pig)
title: Porcine animals are another major source of Animal Cartilage, commonly harvested from the trachea, ribs, or joint tissues of pigs, alongside Bovine sources in supplement and food ingredient production.

label: Animal Cartilage can be Harvested from Broiler Avian (Poultry/Fowls)
title: Avian Cartilage is primarily sourced from farmed Poultry such as Chickens, Turkeys, and Ducks, typically from joints, the sternum, or the trachea, and is used to a lesser extent than Bovine or Porcine Cartilage.

label: Contain similar compositions
title: Shark Cartilage is a specific type of Animal Cartilage sourced from Sharks.

label: Shark Cartilage can be Harvested from Sharks and Elasmobranchs
title: Shark Cartilage is obtained from the skeletal structures and fins of Elasmobranchs, including sharks, rays, and skates. It is processed as a high-value material, mainly for niche Nutraceutical and Cosmetic applications.

label: Beef (Bovine Meat) can be Harvested from Bovine (Cow)
title: Beef is the meat derived from farmed Bovine animals, primarily cattle, and is one of the most widely consumed animal protein sources globally.

label: Pork (Pig Meat) can be Harvested from Porcine (Pig)
title: Pork is the primary meat product obtained from farmed Porcine animals (pigs) and is widely used across fresh, processed, and cured food products.

label: Poultry (Avian Meat) can be Harvested from Layer Poultry
title: Meat from spent Layer Hens is often used in low-grade meat products like Soups, Stocks, or Pet Food.

label: Poultry (Avian Meat) can be Harvested from Broiler Avian (Poultry/Fowls)
title: Poultry Meat—such as chicken, turkey, and duck—is harvested from farmed Avian species and represents a major global source of animal protein.

label: Bovine Bones can be Harvested from Bovine (Cow)
title: Bovine Bones are derived from farmed Bovine (Cows/Cattle), primarily as byproducts of the meat and dairy industries.
